cards.text:
The Role of Participants in the Marketplace
All individuals participate in markets as both producers and consumers. Producers contribute through sectors such as agriculture, industry, and services.

Consumers' Role in the Market
Consumers buy goods and services they need, obtaining final products for personal use.

The Need for Rules and Regulations
Regulations are necessary to ensure development, protect workers' rights, and maintain environmental standards.

Exploitation and Unfair Practices
Unfair practices in markets include selling goods below required standards, misleading advertising, and unmentioned extra charges.

Weak Bargaining Position of Consumers
Individual consumers often find themselves in weak positions with sellers who refuse responsibility for sold products.

Initiatives for Consumer Protection
Organizations and movements have emerged to protect consumers against exploitation in the marketplace.

Power Imbalance in the Market
Large companies can manipulate markets due to their wealth and power, while consumers remain scattered and less influential.

False Advertising in the Marketplace
Some companies use incorrect claims in their advertising, misinforming consumers about product benefits.

Case of Infant Formula Misinformation
For years, a company falsely promoted powdered milk over mother's milk, requiring prolonged legal battles to rectify.

Legal Struggles for Truth in Advertising
Consumer protection efforts include long court cases to correct misleading claims, such as those regarding cigarettes and health.

Origins of the Consumer Movement
The consumer movement emerged from consumer dissatisfaction with unfair practices by sellers. This arose due to the lack of a legal system protecting consumers from marketplace exploitation.

Individual Consumer Response
Consumers previously responded to unfair practices by avoiding certain brands or shops, due to the absence of any adequate complaint system.

Consumer Awareness Initiatives
India, like other countries, took years to build consumer awareness and started to shift the quality assurance responsibility from consumers to sellers.

Rise as a Social Force
The consumer movement began in India as a social force to protect interests against unethical trade practices including:
  • Food shortages
  • Hoarding and black marketing
  • Adulteration of consumables

Consumer Groups in the 1970s
Consumer organizations engaged mainly in writing articles, holding exhibitions, and forming groups to investigate:
  • Irregularities in ration shops
  • Passenger transport issues

Growth of Consumer Groups
The consumer movement in India saw significant growth in the number of consumer advocacy groups over time.

Consumers International
Established based on the UN Guidelines for Consumer Protection in 1985, this acted as a global initiative, serving as an umbrella for over 200 member organizations across more than 100 nations.

Pressure on Businesses and Governments
Due to consumer movement efforts, business misconduct was challenged, compelling businesses and governments to improve practices favoring consumer interests.

Consumer Protection Act 1986 (COPRA)
A milestone for consumer rights in India, COPRA was implemented to protect consumer interests and establish legal recourse for grievances.

The United Nations Role in Consumer Rights
The adoption of the UN Guidelines for Consumer Protection was pivotal in aiding both nations and advocacy groups to uphold and press for consumer rights globally.

Consumer Rights: Ensuring Safety
Reji's case highlights a hospital's negligence during anesthesia causing lifelong effects. Consumers have a right to be protected against hazardous goods and services.

Importance of Safety Regulations
Producers must adhere to safety standards, ensuring products like pressure cookers have effective safety valves. Lack of enforcement leads to poor quality products in the market.

Understanding Product Information
When purchasing products, important details include:
  • Ingredients
  • Price and MRP (Maximum Retail Price)
  • Batch and expiry dates
  • Manufacturer's address
Consumers can seek compensation for defective products if necessary.

Role of RTI (Right to Information) Act
The 2005 Act allows consumers to obtain information about government services, empowering people, as seen in Amritha's successful application.

Right to Choose
Consumers have the right to choose services or products without restrictions. Unfair practices like forced purchases deny consumer choice, which is a violation of their rights.

Consumer Justice System
Consumers can approach redressal systems for justice:
  • District Consumer Disputes Redressal Commission: Claims up to Rs 1 crore
  • State Commission: Rs 1 crore to Rs 10 crore
  • National Commission: Claims exceeding Rs 10 crore

Case of Abirami
Abirami was refunded Rs 28,000 from an institute for inadequate teaching. Her case emphasizes the consumer's right to quality education and informed consent.

Role of Consumer Forums
Consumer organizations help individuals file complaints and may engage in legal representation. These forums receive government support to raise awareness.

Understanding Consumer Protections
COPRA has established a three-tier system for consumer dispute redressal, ensuring fair representation and justice for consumers at various levels.

Becoming Informed Consumers
Consumers must be knowledgeable about their rights and services, leading to informed purchasing decisions and conscious consumerism.

National Consumers' Day
India observes National Consumers’ Day on 24 December to commemorate the enactment of the Consumer Protection Act in 1986.

Consumer Protection Act (COPRA)
The Consumer Protection Act was enacted in 1986 and amended in 2019 to strengthen consumer rights further by including online purchases and enabling severe penalties for service deficiencies.

Consumer Redressal in India
  • India has exclusive authority for consumer redressal with specialized commissions.
  • The redressal process can be cumbersome, needing lawyers and requiring time for proceedings.
  • Neutral mediation is encouraged for dispute settlement in all three Consumer Commission levels.

Consumer Groups in India
Over 2000 consumer groups exist, with only 50-60 being well-organized and recognized.

Challenges in Consumer Redressal
  • Many retail purchases are too small to issue cash memos, making evidence gathering challenging.
  • Engagement of lawyers adds to time and costs.

Consumer Awareness
Consumer awareness is slowly spreading, but requires increased participation and voluntary efforts to be effective.

Improvements with COPRA
  • Expansion to include online purchases.
  • Penalties applied for service deficiencies.
  • Mediator role introduced for dispute settlement.

Shortcomings in Market Regulations
Laws protecting workers, particularly in unorganized sectors, are weakly enforced; market rules are frequently ignored.

Role of Consumers
Emphasize the importance of active consumer involvement and the realization of their roles to make consumer movements effective.

Consumer Movement Progress
  • Progress made in terms of organized groups.
  • Approximately 2000 groups with some recognized for their impactful work.
